title: Pretoria
subtitle: 12-Jun-40
text: Dave’s signed Oath of Attestation records his voluntary enlistment into full-time service with the Union Defence Force, where he swore allegiance to King George VI of England at the age of 23. Until that moment, he had been employed as a bus driver with the Pretoria City Council.
caption: Army cards

title: Durban
subtitle: 21-Apr-41
text: This is the SS Dempo in Liverpool Dock, England in March 1941, while being converted from a cruise liner to a troop carrier. Dave embarked on her at Durban, South Africa, on 21 April 1941.
caption: Army cards

title: Port Suez
subtitle: 5-Jun-41
text: When they disembarked at Port Suez on 6 May 1941, each soldier was issued with a “dog tag.” This is the one Dave wore every day throughout his five years of service. It recorded his personal details and his wishes for burial, with “W.E.S.” indicating his faith as Wesleyan Methodist.
caption: Army cards

title: Cairo
subtitle: 1-Sep-41
text: In Sep 1941 the 8th Army headquarters was established in Cairo, and Dave was assigned to the Eighth Army- This is his uniform Clasp
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Alexandria
subtitle: 10-Sep-41
text: During a short visit to Alexandria, before heading into battle Dave sent this photo to his mom. She noted on the back that she  "received it from Alexandria on 22/11/41" in Pretoria, South Africa
caption: Photo to Mom

title: El Alemein
subtitle: 25-Nov-41
text: The Operation Crusader uniform patches Dave wore during the build-up of supply lines and the winning of the first battle at El Alemain. The objective of the campaign was to retake Libya and relieve the besieged port of Tobruk from the Italian-German -Axis- forces under General Erwin Rommel. This objective was achieved by 25 Nov 1941.(The hand stitching of his uniform badges indicate that they were made in Egypt and worn in battle)
caption: Crusader Badge

title: Bir Hacheim
subtitle: 4-Feb-42
text: The 8th Army advanced and re-grouped here to rest and re-stock supplies. Daves uniform badge with crossed rifles indicate that he was a trained infantryman (rifleman) serving in a front-line unit with the 27th Road Construction division of the 8th Army.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Bir Hacheim
subtitle: 19-May-42
text: Daves letter posted home to his mom via APO (Army Post Office) on 19/5/1942
caption: Letter to mom

title: Gazala
subtitle: 30-Jun-42
text: On 26 May 1942, the Battle of Gazala began. This time the Eighth Army was defeated. Tobruk fell, and 32,000 Allied troops were captured. The Eighth Army retreated eastward and by 30 June 1942, Rommel's Axis forces had pushed the 8th army back to El Alamein again. Here Allied troops are again offloading water to re-stock supplies
caption: 8th Army Clasp
image: images/18_Solum Bay Offloading water for supply lines.jpg

title: El Alemein
subtitle: 11-Nov-42
text: Months later, on 23 October 1942, the Second Battle of El Alamein began — the battle that would change everything and last until 11Nov 1942. Under the new command of Lieutenant Montgomery (Seen here in a tank in North Africa in Nov 1942) , the Eighth Army launched a massive offensive and won.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Mursa Matruh
subtitle: 20-Nov-42
text: Mursa Matruh:Following the Allied victory at El Alamein, Axis forces retreated rapidly. The British Eighth Army reoccupied Mersa Matruh with only light resistance. Here Gen Jan Smuts visits the SA troops in their trademark "Tin Hats"

title: Sidi Barrani
subtitle: 1-Dec-42
text: Sidi Barrani : Small-scale but tactically important, this battle was  designed to slow the Allied advance and involvied land mines, artillery, and brief armoured clashes
image: images/24_Tanks used by the allied troops in the retreat from Tobruk in 1941..jpg

title: El Agheila
subtitle: 20-Dec-42
text: El Agheila: This was the first serious defensive stand after the retreat at El Alemain . Rommel attempted to delay the British advance. The British outflanked the position, forcing another withdrawal. Here Rommel is seen instructing his troops in North Africa.

title: Tripoli
subtitle: 1-Jan-43
text: Tripoli:Continued pressure by the British Eighth Army. Axis forces avoided encirclement but kept retreating. Tripoli fell to the Allies on 23 January 1943.
caption: Tripoli

title: El-Hammah
subtitle: 20-Mar-43
text: On this date, Allied forces (primarily elements of the British Eighth Army) launched probing and preliminary attacks against Axis positions around El Hammah. This action was essentially a testing engagement, helping Montgomery assess the strength of the Mareth Line defences.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Wādī al-ʿAkārīt
subtitle: 6-Apr-43
text: After retreating from the Battle of the Mareth Line, Axis forces made their stand here. At Wādī al-ʿAkārīt (often called the Battle of Akarit) Fighting was intense, especially on key ridges . Despite heavy resistance, Allied troops broke through the defensive line. Here German troops (recognised by their helmet shape) are seen struggling through sand and high winds
caption: 8th Army Clasp
image: images/32_German Soldiers struggling against wind and sand.jpg

title: Enfidha
subtitle: 20-Apr-43
text:
This battle took place near the town of Enfidha. Despite determined attacks, Allied forces made limited progress and suffered significant casualties.
The battle became largely a stalemate, with heavy fighting continuing for several days.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Medjerda
subtitle: 6-May-43
text:
The fighting occurred along the Medjerda River valley. The Allied forces broke through Axis defenses along the Medjerda. This advance contributed directly to the fall of Tunis on 7 May 1943.
Within days, all Axis forces in North Africa surrendered (around 13 May 1943).
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Tunis
subtitle: 15-May-43
text: Journey back to Cairo: After the fall of Tunis in May 1943, the men of the 27th Road Construction Unit began the long return eastward toward Cairo;a journey that was as demanding as any campaign, though far less recorded.
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Gabes
subtitle: 24-May-43
text: Journey back to Cairo: (Tunis to Gabes 270km)  Much of the route retraced the scarred coastline of North Africa, following the same desert roads they had helped to build and maintain during the advance. The men marched through the heat of the day and the cold of the desert nights, often carrying their own gear when transport columns were stretched thin. Progress depended on fuel, serviceable vehicles, and the condition of the roads
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Tripoli
subtitle: 27-May-43
text: Journey back to Cairo: (Gabes to Tripoli 380km) Passing back through Tripoli, Benghazi, and Tobruk, the journey became a slow unwinding of the campaign itself. What had once been a desperate westward push was now a weary return,
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: El Agheila
subtitle: 30-May-43
text: Journey back to Cairo: (Tripoli to El Agheila 480km) They went back along the very roads they had once laboured to keep open under fire.
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Benghazi
subtitle: 31-May-43
text: Journey back to Cairo:(El Agheila to Benghazi 280km) The road marked by abandoned vehicles, shattered positions, and long stretches of silence broken only by and sand.
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Tobruk
subtitle: 15-Jun-43
text: Journey back to Cairo: (Benghazi to Tobruk 400km) By truck where vehicles were available, and on foot when they were not, the unit moved in stages through a landscape still marked by war
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Mersa Matruh
subtitle: 20-Jun-43
text: Journey back to Cairo:(Tobruk to Mursa Matruh 360km) Water was rationed carefully, and halts were taken where wells or supply points could be found.
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Alexandria
subtitle: 25-Jun-43
text: Journey back to Cairo:(Mursa Matruh to Alexanderia 260km) By the time they reached Alexandria and finally Cairo, the journey had become a test of stamina and discipline rather than combat
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Cairo
subtitle: 30-Jun-43
text: For his service in the 8th Army Dave had earned this Africa Star
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Khatatba
subtitle: 8-Jul-43
text: For his service in Africa, Dave had earned this  Africa Service Medal
caption: 27th Rd Con Unit

title: Port Suez
subtitle: 12-Jul-43
text: Dave embarked at Port Suez for Durban on The Home Exchange Program. During this leave he had his Photo taken at a Pretoria photographer - Wearing his Operation crusader uniform Badge
caption: Army cards

title: Port Suez
subtitle: 6-Nov-43
text: On 6 November 1943 Dave disembarked from SS Duchess of Richmond (from Durban) after Home Leave, having stopped at Madagascar where Dave was able to get this 5Franc note and keep it as a souvenir
caption: Army cards

title: Cairo
subtitle: 16-Dec-43
text: He was transferred to Citizen Military to prepare to leave for Italy, and he bought an Egypt keepsake
caption: Army cards

title: Helwan
subtitle: 1-Jan-44
text: Dave was admitted to Hospital No5 after Accidental Injury-possible motor bike accident. In June 1943 he requested a transfer to the South African Engineering Corps as a Sapper. This lowered his rank, but family stories suggest that it allowed him to become, an unofficial, a dispatch rider. Here is an example of the bikes that were used. The riders were expected to maintain and repair the bikes in all conditions
caption: Army cards

title: Alexandria
subtitle: 27-May-44
text: Discharged from Hospital to Base and again prepared to leave for Italy
caption: Army cards

title: Reggio de Calabria
subtitle: 1-Jun-44
text: 8th Army troops landed at this port from Alexandria, and so did Dave ( He had witnessed the Eruption of Mount Etna on his voyage) Because he had been in Hospital in Cairo when his unit went to Italy, he caught up with his fellow soldiers (with their trademark Tin Hats)for the first time in Sep 1944 near Rimini (Coriano)
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Coriano
subtitle: 13-Sep-44
text: The Battle of Coriano was a hard-fought engagement during the Allied advance through northern Italy, forming part of the larger offensive against the Gothic Line which had been defensively set up by the Germans where the area was sown with land mines, laced with barbed wire, and covered by carefully positioned artillery and machine-gun fire.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Emelia Romano Region
subtitle: 1-Mar-45
text: The Allied Army Base was in the Emelia Romano Region where Allied soldiers prepared the pontoon bridge they used to cross the Po river (This picture is from an article in Dave's copy of the 8th Army Newspaper). Both side had to wait out the Italian winter between Oct 1944 and Mar 1945.
caption: Italy visit
image: images/76_Pontoon bridge over the Po River- from 8Army News.jpg

title: Po River at Ferara
subtitle: 28-Apr-45
text: Dave kept this leaflet which was handed out to soldiers for encouragement to push accross the Po River. The Po River was the last major natural barrier in Italy. Allied engineers launched rapid assault crossings using boats and pontoon bridges.Crossing points were established near Ferrara and other sectors. Air superiority from the Allied Air Forces disrupted German withdrawal routes
caption: Propaganda leaflet
image: images/78_Po River is waiting for You 20.jpg

title: Venice
subtitle: 2-May-45
text: Crossing the Po River marked the end of organized resistance in Italy. What followed was a swift collapse of the German defensive line. The Italian campaign became a swift, decisive sweep to the Alps, ending in total collapse. This postcard from Dave's collection marks Venice as the town reached soon after crossing the Po River
caption: Post cards

title: Lignano Sabbia d'Ora
subtitle: 4-May-45
text: The Eighth Army proceeded northeast toward Trieste. meeting only minor skirmishs along the way. This postcard from Lignano Sabbia d'Ora was from next town on Dave's journey
caption: Post cards

title: Udine
subtitle: 6-May-45
text: Dave collected postcards from every town he passed through—without a camera, they were his only way of capturing and preserving those moments. This is the railway station in Udine
caption: Post cards

title: Trieste
subtitle: 8-May-45
text: When the 8th Army entered Trieste the end of the war was in sight. They didn’t enter Trieste to fight a battle, but to win the peace. They had to secure the strategic city, prevent Yugoslav annexation and establish Allied control at a critical political moment. Dave remembered it by this postcard
caption: Post cards

title: Trieste
subtitle: 8-May-45
text: Germany OUT! A newspaper Dave bought on 8 May 1945 in Trieste. News also broke that Benito Mussolini was executed by his fellow Italians on 28 April 1945 , and Adolf Hitler committed suicide on 30 April 1945 . The war was over
caption: Newspaper

title: Cairo
subtitle: 8-May-45
text: The Medal : the Star of Italy was awarded to Dave for his time served in Italy
caption: Medal

title: Cairo-Emplaned for Union
subtitle: 8-Aug-45
text: Demobilised - end of war - Dave earned these 3 medals for his service throughout the war
caption: Army cards

title: Nairobi
subtitle: 9-Aug-45
text: His flight on the way home to South Africa, stopped  in Nairobi to re-fuel,  Dave bought this newspaper, and learned that the Atom Bomb had been dropped at Hiroshima.
caption: newspaper

title: Pretoria
subtitle: 11-Dec-46
text: Back in Pretoria, South Africa Dave returned to his job as Bus Driver for the Pretoria City Council, and married Margaret Clara Orr on 11 December 1946
caption: marriage Cert
